plainMeaning: A good Jain layperson stays calm, thinks clearly, holds back wrongdoing, speaks carefully, is kind to every kind of living thing, keeps company with religious people, masters the senses, and lets the heart settle into right conduct.
recitation: Uvasama vivega saṁvara, bhāsā-samiï chajjīva-karuṇā ya; dhammia-jaṇa-saṁsaggo, karaṇa-damo caraṇa-pariṇāmo.

wordByWord.gloss: calm; the quieting of the passions (anger, pride, deceit, greed)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit from Skt. upaśama, 'subsidence', from upa- + √śam ('to grow calm'); cf. the upaśama-bhāva of the kaṣāyas.
wordByWord.notes: First of the eight inner duties enumerated in this verse (śrāvaka-duty ~26).

wordByWord.gloss: discernment; discriminating the soul (jīva) from the non-soul and the right from the harmful
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit from Skt. viveka, from vi- + √vic ('to separate, distinguish').
wordByWord.notes: The faculty that makes saṁvara possible — one cannot stop what one cannot tell apart.

wordByWord.gloss: stopping the inflow of karma (glossed in preface)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit from Skt. saṁvara, from sam- + √vṛ ('to cover, close, restrain').
wordByWord.notes: Used here as a lay practice; doctrinally the counter-pole of āsrava (Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.1).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit; Skt. bhāṣā-samiti = bhāṣā ('speech') + samiti ('careful regulation', from sam- + √i).
wordByWord.notes: The single samiti singled out for the householder, who speaks far more than the silent ascetic.

wordByWord.gloss: compassion toward the six classes of living beings (glossed in preface)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit chaj-jīva (Skt. ṣaḍ-jīva, 'six [classes of] life') + karuṇā ('compassion'); the six = earth-, water-, fire-, air-, plant- and mobile-bodied beings.
wordByWord.notes: The active, outward face of the inner restraint named in the same line.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit conjunction from Skt. ca, here in its enclitic Prakrit form ya.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the first half-line, binding the items into one list.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhammia (Skt. dhārmika, 'religious, righteous') + jaṇa (Skt. jana, 'people') + saṁsaggo (Skt. saṁsarga, 'contact, association', from sam- + √sṛj).
wordByWord.notes: Masculine nominative singular -o ending. The one social item in an otherwise interior verse — virtue is sustained by good company (satsaṅga).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit karaṇa (Skt. karaṇa, 'instrument', here the sense-organs/indriyas) + damo (Skt. dama, 'restraint, taming', from √dam).
wordByWord.notes: Synonymous with indriya-dama / indriya-nigraha; the disciplining of the five senses.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit caraṇa (Skt. caraṇa, 'conduct, cāritra') + pariṇāmo (Skt. pariṇāma, 'transformation, disposition', from pari- + √nam).
wordByWord.notes: Read by some as the inward yearning for ascetic conduct/dīkṣā; the climactic item, since it is the inner inclination from which all the rest flow.

literalTranslation: Calm, discernment, restraint of karmic inflow, carefulness of speech, and compassion to the six classes of living beings; association with religious people, control of the senses, (and) a disposition ripening toward right conduct.
idiomaticTranslation: Inner calm, clear discernment, the closing-off of karma's inflow, guarded speech, and tender compassion for every order of living being; the keeping of pious company, the mastery of the senses, and a heart that has turned, of itself, toward right conduct.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where verse 3 sent the devotee outward — into worship, almsgiving and pilgrimage — this verse turns the gaze wholly inward, naming eight states that the earlier acts were meant to produce. Its architecture is causal, not merely a list. It opens with upaśama, the settling of the four passions; only a quieted mind can exercise vivega, the discernment that sorts soul from non-soul and benefit from harm; and only such discernment yields saṁvara, the actual shutting of karma's doors. The poet then gives saṁvara two concrete faces a householder can practise all day: bhāṣā-samiti, watched speech (chosen, of the five samitis, because the layperson lives by talking), and chajjīva-karuṇā, compassion extended even to the one-sensed earth-, water-, fire-, air- and plant-bodied beings — so restraint is shown to be inseparable from active kindness. The second half-line guards and completes the work. Dhammia-jaṇa-saṁsaggo — pious company — is the lone outward item, the social scaffolding without which the inner virtues erode; karaṇa-damo, sense-mastery, is the daily discipline that protects saṁvara; and the verse crests on caraṇa-pariṇāmo, a mind that has so ripened that right conduct is no longer effortful imposition but its own settled inclination. The movement is therefore from a calm mind, through discriminating restraint and its compassionate expression, to a self whose very disposition has become conduct — the interior summit of the householder's path, just before verse 5 widens out again to the Saṅgha and the scriptures.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse compresses the canonical mechanics of liberation into lay practice. Saṁvara is the technical counter to āsrava (karmic influx): in the Tattvārtha-sūtra it is achieved by gupti, samiti, dharma, anuprekṣā, parīṣaha-jaya and cāritra — and this gāthā quietly names members of that very apparatus (samiti via bhāṣā-samiti, the seed of cāritra in caraṇa-pariṇāma, restraint in karaṇa-dama). Viveka here is bheda-vijñāna, the discriminative knowledge that Jain soteriology makes the hinge of all progress, since restraint without discernment is mere suppression. Karaṇa-dama belongs to the indriya-nigraha that both ascetic and śrāvaka share, and chajjīva-karuṇā grounds the whole in ahiṁsā toward the ṣaḍ-jīva-nikāya, refusing any restraint that is privately tranquil yet indifferent to other life. The closing caraṇa-pariṇāma marks the doctrinal point that conduct (cāritra) is ultimately a pariṇāma — a transformation of the soul's own disposition — not a set of external acts; the lay items of the prior verses matter precisely because they cultivate this inner bent.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For today's reciter the verse reads as a daily inner audit running beneath outward observance: Did I keep my temper subsided? Did I watch my words and my keystrokes? Was I needlessly careless of small lives — insects, plants, water? Whose company am I keeping, and is it steadying or eroding me? It pairs an achievable social lever — deliberately seeking out dhārmika company — with a private one — reining in the senses — and reassures the practitioner that the goal is not grim self-policing but caraṇa-pariṇāma, the day when right conduct finally feels like one's own natural leaning rather than a rule obeyed.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.1–9.6 (saṁvara; its means: gupti, samiti, dharma, etc.)
crossReferences.source: Umāsvāti, tr. N. Tatia, That Which Is

crossReferences.text: Bhāṣā-samiti as one of the five samitis

sources.title: Manaha Jinanam Sajjay — Jain Sutras and Stotras (text + 36-duty explanation)
sources.consultedFor: verbatim verse-4 reading and the mapping of its eight terms to śrāvaka-duties 26–33 (be peaceful, discretion, stop karmic inflow, watchful speech, compassion, pious company, sense-control, inclination to conduct/renunciation)

sources.title: Can you give the whole Manaha Jinanam Anam sutra in English
sources.consultedFor: independent IAST transliteration of verse 4 confirming token boundaries (uvasama-vivega-saṁvara, bhāsā-samīī chajīva-karuṇā ya, dhammia-jaṇa-saṁsaggo, karaṇa-damo caraṇa-pariṇāmo)

sources.title: Samvara — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: doctrinal placement of saṁvara as the stoppage of karmic influx (āsrava) and its standard means in the Tattvārtha tradition

notes: The verse is an enumerative half-gāthā pair; every token is glossed. caraṇa-pariṇāmo is genuinely ambiguous: lexically 'a disposition ripening toward conduct (cāritra)', but the printed Anchalgachchha-adjacent tradition (jainknowledge) renders the corresponding duty as 'desire for renunciation/dīkṣā' — both senses are given. Orthography follows the source verbatim, including ભાસાસમિઈ (single-spelled samiï) and the compound hyphenation; the cited transliteration sources show minor spelling variants (chajjīva vs chajīva, bhāsā-samiï vs bhāsā-samīī) which do not affect meaning. The doctrinal cross-references (Tattvārtha, Uttarādhyayana, Daśavaikālika) are standard Jain loci for the concepts named, not claims that this gāthā quotes them.
